document.docAuthor: Guðmundsson, Guðmundur, Cedhagen, Tomas & Andersen, Tom
document.masterDocTitle: Taxonomy and distribution of recent species of the subfamily Nodosariinae (Foraminifera) in Icelandic waters

mods:titleInfo.mods:title: Taxonomy and distribution of recent species of the subfamily Nodosariinae (Foraminifera) in Icelandic waters

mods:name.mods:affiliation: Icelandic Institute of Natural History, Urridaholtsstraeti 6 - 8, IS- 210 Gardabaer, Iceland. Aarhus University, Department of Biology, Section of Aquatic Biology, Building 1135, Ole Worms allé 1, DK- 8000 Aarhus C, Denmark. University of Oslo, Department of Biosciences, The faculty of Mathematics and Natural Sciences, P. O. Box 1066 Blindern, N- 0316 Oslo, Norway.

bibRefCitation.author: d'Orbigny A. D.
bibRefCitation.refString: d'Orbigny A. D. 1846. Die fossilen Foraminiferen des tertiaren Beckens von Wien. Foraminiferes fossiles du bassin tertiaire de Vienne. Guide et Comp, Paris. https: // doi. org / 10.5962 / bhl. title. 145432

figureCitation.captionText: Fig. 2. Dentalina antennula d’Orbigny, 1846. A, D. Microsphere (BIOICE 2398; IINH 40362) side view (A), and aperture (D). B–C. Megalosphere (BIOICE 2222, IINH 40365), side view (B) and aperture (C), stained with indigo blue. Light source incident light and dark field. Scale bars = 0.25 mm.

Dentalina antennula
–

, pl. 15 figs 7–9. —

, pl. 1 fig. p, pl. 2, fig. c.

•
5 ; 63.7567° N , 25.7097° W ; RP sledge; depth
366 m
; 7.1°C; 35.15 ppt;
11 Sep. 2003
; BIOICE 3608 ; IINH 40385
.

Test shape elongate, cylindrical, slightly curved to straight, moderately nodular; initial end rounded in megalospheres and only slightly tapering; the microsphere is gradually tapering to a pointed initial end. Length of test 4–5 mm , the largest 7 mm ; test width 0.3–0.5 mm . Chambers initially globular to subglobular, becoming slightly more elongated as added; rectilinearly arranged in both generations; number of chambers 5–9 in megalospheres, 15–21 in microspheres. Chamber embracement minimal, sutures horizontal. Aperture radial, nearly central, slightly raised, sometimes protruding, with up to 12 symmetric tines that are fused in the center ( Fig. 2C–D ). Short costae are confined to base of chambers; initial end usually with one or more basal knobs or a short spine. Secondary surface laminations absent (atelo-lamellar). Wall medium thick, finely perforated.

Of the 63 examined specimens, 22 were microspheres. Diameter of proloculus in the megalospheres is about 0.5 mm , but less than 0.03 mm in the microsphere. The illustration of the lectotype of
D. antennula
shows an open, rounded and crenulate aperture, but they also questioned the validity of this species, stating “that the independence of this species remains doubtful” ( Papp & Schmid 1985 ). Judging from the illustrations it seems that the tip is worn away and it is assumed here that it originally had fused apertural tines as the present specimens. Several names of fossil forms have been erected that seem very similar to
D. antennula
, like
Dentalina capitata ( Boll, 1846 )
described from the Oligocene by Boll (1846: 177 , pl. 2 fig. 13a–b);
Dentalina buchi
from the Eocene described by Reuss (1851: 60 , pl. 3 fig. 6); and
Dentalina philippii
also from the Eocene and erected by Reuss (1851: 60 , pl. 3, fig. 5).
